---
title: Overview
bibliography: ../references.bib
---

::: {.callout-tip}
## Reading

@jurafsky_speech_2023[Ch. 2.5] on edit distance and string alignment.
:::

In the [previous module](../uncertainty-about-languages/index.qmd), I said that we would investigate two complementary ways of dealing with the fact that we don't always know which strings are in a language. The first was to model the gradation in acceptability judgments as a kind of *uncertainty about what is in the language*—assigning probabilities to strings using language models. We developed that perspective through [probability theory](../uncertainty-about-languages/what-is-a-probability.qmd), [$N$-gram models](../uncertainty-about-languages/ngram-models.qmd), and [various approaches to context abstraction](../uncertainty-about-languages/beyond-ngrams.qmd).

The second perspective, which we turn to now, is different in character. Rather than asking "how probable is this string under a model of the language?", it asks "how similar is this string to strings we already know?" Under this view, the acceptability of a novel string is not a property of the string in isolation—it is a function of the string's relationship to other strings in the lexicon. A nonword like *blick* is judged as wordlike not because some grammar assigns it high probability, but because it is close to many real English words (*black*, *block*, *brick*, *lick*, *click*, ...). A nonword like *bnick* is judged as un-wordlike because it is far from most of the lexicon.

This perspective requires two things that the probabilistic perspective did not: a notion of *distance* between strings, and a way of aggregating distances between a novel string and the strings in a known lexicon. In this module, we develop both.

We'll start with the general concept of distance—what it means for two objects to be close or far—and then specialize to strings, where the standard notion of distance is *edit distance*: the minimum number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions needed to transform one string into another. We'll implement the Levenshtein distance algorithm and the related concept of string alignment. Then, in the final section of this module, we'll put these tools to work: we'll define *neighborhood density* models that predict acceptability from a string's distance to the lexicon, building on the exemplar-based tradition in psycholinguistics [@luce1998recognizing; @nosofsky1986attention], and we'll compare this approach directly against the probabilistic approach from the previous module, following the methodology of @bailey2001determinants and @vitevitch1999probabilistic.

The two perspectives—probability-based and similarity-based—are not mutually exclusive, and there is a long-standing debate about their relative contributions to phonotactic knowledge [see @bailey2001determinants; @albright2009feature for discussion]. One of the things we'll be able to do at the end of this module is bring data to bear on that question.
